title: Chambly, Quebec

infobox.value: [J3L](/wiki/List_of_J_postal_codes_of_Canada "List of J postal codes of Canada")

(French pronunciation: [[ʃɑ̃bli]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is an [off-island suburb](/wiki/Greater_Montreal "Greater Montreal") of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") in southwestern [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), Canada. It is located in the [Montérégie](/wiki/Mont%C3%A9r%C3%A9gie "Montérégie") region, inland from the [South Shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Montreal) "South Shore (Montreal)") of the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River").

**Chambly** (French pronunciation: [[ʃɑ̃bli]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is an [off-island suburb](/wiki/Greater_Montreal "Greater Montreal") of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") in southwestern [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), Canada. It is located in the [Montérégie](/wiki/Mont%C3%A9r%C3%A9gie "Montérégie") region, inland from the [South Shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Montreal) "South Shore (Montreal)") of the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River").

It was formed from the merger in 1965 of Fort-Chambly (formerly Chambly-Canton prior to 1952) and the old city of Chambly (formerly Chambly-Basin prior to 1952, and earlier sometimes called Bassin-de-Chambly).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Panneau_signalant_le_parc_Jacques-De_Chambly%2C_Chambly.jpg/250px-Panneau_signalant_le_parc_Jacques-De_Chambly%2C_Chambly.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panneau_signalant_le_parc_Jacques-De_Chambly,_Chambly.jpg)

Sign indicating Jacques-De Chambly park, rue Martel, Chambly

Descendants of European immigrants have lived in Chambly since the 17th century, but Chambly was not incorporated as a city until 1965.

[Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") passed through the area that came to be the site of the town of Chambly, QC, in 1609., when he wrote the following in his journal:

> The approach to the rapids is a sort of lake into which the water flows down, and it is about three leagues in circumference. Nearby are meadows where no Indians live, by reason of the wars. At the rapids there is very little water, but it flows with great swiftness, and there are many rocks and boulders, so that the Indians cannot go up by water; but on the way back they run them very nicely. All this region is very level and full of forests, vines and butternut trees. No Christian has ever visited this land and we had all the misery of the world trying to paddle the river upstream.

[Fort Chambly](/wiki/Fort_Chambly "Fort Chambly") was captured by American forces on October 20, 1775, during the [American Invasion of Canada of 1775–76](/wiki/Invasion_of_Canada_(1775) "Invasion of Canada (1775)"), it was held until the spring of 1776 when it was evacuated and burned, as the Americans retreated southward to [Fort Ticonderoga](/wiki/Fort_Ticonderoga "Fort Ticonderoga"). Subsequently, prisoners-of-war from the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army"), including Colonel [William Stacy](/wiki/William_Stacy "William Stacy"), were held at Fort Chambly until the end of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War").

During the occupation the [1st Canadian Regiment](/wiki/1st_Canadian_Regiment "1st Canadian Regiment"), an [Extra Continental regiment](/wiki/Extra_Continental_regiments_and_Additional_Continental_regiments "Extra Continental regiments and Additional Continental regiments"), was raised by [James Livingston](/wiki/James_Livingston_(American_Revolution) "James Livingston (American Revolution)") to support Colonial efforts in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") during the [invasion of Quebec](/wiki/Invasion_of_Canada_(1775) "Invasion of Canada (1775)"). Livingston recruited men from Chambly, [Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763-1791) "Province of Quebec (1763-1791)") as early as September 1775,[[5]](#cite_note-5) but a formal regimental designation was made by [Richard Montgomery](/wiki/Richard_Montgomery "Richard Montgomery") on November 20, 1775, with recognition by the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") following on January 8, 1776. The regiment, which never approached its authorized size of 1,000 men, saw action primarily in the Canadian theater and New York, and was disbanded on January 1, 1781.

The College of Chambly was chartered on March 21, 1835, in Lower Canada.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Fort Chambly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Fort Chambly")]

Main article: [Fort Chambly](/wiki/Fort_Chambly "Fort Chambly")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Fort_Chambly_1840.jpg/250px-Fort_Chambly_1840.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Chambly_1840.jpg)

[Fort Chambly](/wiki/Fort_Chambly "Fort Chambly"), sitting on the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River")

Chambly is home to the massive [Fort Chambly](/wiki/Fort_Chambly "Fort Chambly"), built with local stone between 1709 and 1711 in the style of [Vauban](/wiki/Vauban "Vauban")'s classic French fortifications. It was built at the mouth of a large basin, on the site of successive wooden forts dating back to 1665. [Fort Chambly](/wiki/Fort_Chambly "Fort Chambly") was the largest in a series of fortifications on the shores of what was known as the Iroquois River (later known as the Chambly River, finally becoming the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River") in the nineteenth century). Originally called Fort Saint-Louis, it soon came to be known by the name of its first commanding officer, [Jacques de Chambly](/wiki/Jacques_de_Chambly "Jacques de Chambly"), to whom the surrounding seigniory was granted in 1672. It was intended to protect [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") in general (and [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") in specific) from attack from indigenous peoples and the English. Today, the fort is run by [Parks Canada](/wiki/Parks_Canada "Parks Canada") and is designated a [National Historic Site of Canada](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada "National Historic Sites of Canada"), and houses a museum and interpretive centre, and hosts historical re-enactments of military drills (as well as a number of contemporary cultural events).

A small local population clustered around the fort, and the entire area eventually became known as Chambly as well. Among the buildings around the Fort was [St. Stephen's Anglican Church](/wiki/St._Stephen%27s_Anglican_Church "St. Stephen's Anglican Church"), which was built to serve the soldiers in garrison as well as the local Loyalist and English settler population.

### Chambly Canal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Chambly Canal")]

Main article: [Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Ecluse_de_Chambly.jpg/250px-Ecluse_de_Chambly.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ecluse_de_Chambly.jpg)

[Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal") lock

Chambly is also known for the [Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal"), a National Historic Site run by [Parks Canada](/wiki/Parks_Canada "Parks Canada"). It was built in 1843 to bypass several kilometers of successive [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River") rapids between the towns of Chambly, QC, and [Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu](/wiki/Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu "Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu"). Part of a series of waterways connecting the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal") was built to facilitate commercial traffic between [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

Trade dwindled after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and as of the 1970s, traffic has been replaced by recreational vessels. Today the canal is enjoyed by tourists and more than 7,000 pleasure boats in the summer, and ice skaters in the winter.

St-Joseph of Chambly Church, at 164 rue Martel, was built between 1880 and 1881. The parish was founded in 1665.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Eglise_St-Joseph%2C_Chambly%2C_QC.jpg/250px-Eglise_St-Joseph%2C_Chambly%2C_QC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eglise_St-Joseph,_Chambly,_QC.jpg)

Chambly's Church St-Joseph, viewed in winter from the Basin

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geography")]

It sits on the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River") in the [Regional County Municipality of La-Vallée-du-Richelieu](/wiki/La_Vall%C3%A9e-du-Richelieu_Regional_County_Municipality "La Vallée-du-Richelieu Regional County Municipality"), at [45°27′00″N 73°17′27″W﻿ / ﻿45.45000°N 73.29083°W﻿ / 45.45000; -73.29083](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Chambly,_Quebec&params=45_27_00_N_73_17_27_W_).

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Demographics")]

In the [2021 Census of Population](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") conducted by [Statistics Canada](/wiki/Statistics_Canada "Statistics Canada"), Chambly had a population of 31,444 living in 12,405 of its 12,609 total private dwellings, a change of 8% from its 2016 population of 29,120. With a land area of 25.08 km2 (9.68 sq mi), it had a population density of 1,253.7/km2 (3,247.2/sq mi) in 2021.[[7]](#cite_note-2021census-7)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Economy")]

Chambly is home to the annual *Festival Bières et Saveurs de Chambly* (Chambly Beers and Flavours Festival), an annual beer and local food festival that takes place every [Labour Day](/wiki/Labour_Day_(Canada) "Labour Day (Canada)") weekend since 2002. Minors are allowed to attend, but must be accompanied by a parent or guardian at all times. There is a special zone in the festival area dedicated just for minors as well. The festival takes place just outside of Fort Chambly.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Ecluse_de_Chambly%2C_Chambly%2C_QC.jpg/250px-Ecluse_de_Chambly%2C_Chambly%2C_QC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ecluse_de_Chambly,_Chambly,_QC.jpg)

[Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal") locks, viewed in winter

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

The [exo Chambly-Richelieu-Carignan](/wiki/CIT_Chambly-Richelieu-Carignan "CIT Chambly-Richelieu-Carignan") region provides commuter and local bus services.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Education")]

In English, the [South Shore Protestant Regional School Board](/wiki/South_Shore_Protestant_Regional_School_Board "South Shore Protestant Regional School Board")[[12]](#cite_note-12) and later the [Richelieu Valley School Board](/w/index.php?title=Richelieu_Valley_School_Board&action=edit&redlink=1 "Richelieu Valley School Board (page does not exist)")[[13]](#cite_note-13) previously served the municipality. Currently Chambly is served by the [Riverside School Board](/wiki/Riverside_School_Board "Riverside School Board") and specifically by William Latter Elementary School. Anglophone secondary students in Chambly are zoned for [Heritage Regional High School](/wiki/Heritage_Regional_High_School "Heritage Regional High School") in [Longueuil](/wiki/Longueuil "Longueuil")'s [Saint-Hubert](/wiki/Saint-Hubert,_Quebec "Saint-Hubert, Quebec") borough.

In French, the [Commission scolaire des Patriotes](/wiki/Commission_scolaire_des_Patriotes "Commission scolaire des Patriotes") serves Chambly, with the following schools located in the municipality:

* École De Bourgogne (Elementary)
* École De Salaberry (Elementary)
* École Jacques-De Chambly (Elementary)
* École Sainte-Marie (Elementary)
* École Madeleine-Brousseau (Elementary[[14]](#cite_note-14))
* École secondaire de Chambly (Secondary 1, 2 and 3)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly,_Quebec&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Media")]

Chambly is currently served by a local weekly newspaper called the "Journal de Chambly", first published in 1966.

A small daily news sheet called Chambly Matin also maintains a journalistic presence on the internet reporting on local issues.

* [Robert Lebel](/wiki/Robert_Lebel_(ice_hockey) "Robert Lebel (ice hockey)") – Former mayor of Chambly, Hockey Hall of Fame inductee
* [Étienne Lucier](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Lucier "Étienne Lucier") – Fur trader
* [Étienne Provost](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Provost "Étienne Provost") – Fur trader
